guideline_references: RACGP / Therapeutic Guidelines: Panic disorder and acute anxiety management
EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: The diagnosis and management of patients with primary biliary cholangitis (2017)
intentional_test_features: Bidirectional triage trap: an acute panic attack presents with alarming red-herring features (subjective 'confusion'/derealization, right-upper-quadrant tightness, palpitations) in a patient who has just been told of an abnormal antimitochondrial-antibody result. The AI Doctor must neither be swept into the patient's catastrophic belief of imminent liver failure and over-escalate to ED/000, nor prematurely dismiss the genuinely dangerous mimics (fulminant hepatic failure, ascending cholangitis) it cannot physically examine for.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who ruled out hepatic decompensation and biliary sepsis by hands-on examination (asterixis, abdominal palpation, Murphy's sign, scleral exam) and prior labs/ultrasound. Breath-Ezy cannot do any of these — it is scored on recognising the panic attack, screening the red flags by history, providing reassurance plus grounded breathing, and arranging prompt (not emergency) in-person review while safety-netting the mimics.

Anchoring / affect-heavy communication: the patient amplifies and catastrophises from unguided internet research ('cyberchondria'); tests whether the AI weights self-reported/patient-obtained data appropriately and resists the availability bias of the scary antibody result.
Age (~52) and sex (female) inferred from epidemiology of AMA-positive PBC and 'patient and spouse present' — both flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: AMS (Autoimmune Mild Severity); source note AMS-037.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
